questions.question: What is the difference between a variable and a constant?
questions.answer: A variable can have its value changed after initial assignment. A constant is bound to a value that should not change. In Python, constants are a convention (SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE) with no runtime enforcement. In JavaScript, const prevents reassignment of the binding but not mutation of the object. In Java, final prevents reassignment.

questions.question: What are the differences between var, let, and const?
questions.answer: var: function-scoped, hoisted and initialized to undefined, can be redeclared. let: block-scoped, hoisted but NOT initialized (accessing before declaration = ReferenceError / TDZ), cannot be redeclared in same scope. const: block-scoped, TDZ, cannot be reassigned (but object properties can still be mutated). Modern JS: prefer const by default, let when reassignment is needed, never var.

questions.question: What is the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)?
questions.answer: The TDZ is the region from the start of a block scope to the point where a let or const variable is declared. Accessing the variable in this region throws a ReferenceError. This exists because let/const are hoisted (moved to the top of their block) but not initialized — the engine knows the variable will exist but refuses access before the declaration line.

questions.question: Why does typeof null === "object" in JavaScript?
questions.answer: This is a historical bug in the original JavaScript implementation. In the first version of JS, values were represented as tagged 32-bit words, and the null pointer (all zeros) shared the "object" tag. The bug was kept for backward compatibility. The correct check for null is: value === null (strict equality).

questions.question: What are the performance characteristics of high-frequency Primitive Types JS queries?
questions.answer: It depends on the data structure used. Average queries execute in O(1) time when using hash indexes, but can degrade to O(N) linear search if colliding.
